questions.question: In LangGraph, what is a 'node'?
options: A message in the conversation history
A step in the agent workflow, typically a function or agent call
questions.explanation: LangGraph models workflows as graphs. Each node is a unit of work — a function, an LLM call, or an agent — and edges define the transitions between nodes.

questions.question: How do agents in a pipeline communicate results?
options: Via direct HTTP requests between agent processes
Through a shared state object or explicit message passing
By writing to and reading from a shared database only
Via environment variables set between calls
questions.explanation: In LangGraph and similar frameworks, agents typically communicate through a shared state dict that gets passed along the graph, or by explicitly returning structured messages to the next agent.

questions.question: What is the best way to test the full multi-agent pipeline?
options: Test each agent in isolation and assume the pipeline works
Monitor the production deployment and fix issues as they arise
Run it end-to-end with a known input and verify the final output
Check only that the coordinator starts without errors
questions.explanation: Integration testing the pipeline end-to-end with a fixed input lets you verify that data flows correctly between agents and the final output meets expectations.
